title: SC-1 Etching of Niobium and Titanium Nitride Thin Films
authors: Adrián Gutiérrez-Cruz, K. A. C. Rathnathilaka, Jani M. Taskinen, Tuomas Vaimala, Kestutis Grigoras, Harshad Mishra, Rishabh Upadhyay, Jorden Senior, Alberto Ronzani
abstract: Dry etching techniques, ubiquitous in microelectronics fabrication, often result in challenging levels of undesired collateral plasma-induced damage. In this work, we demonstrate a wet etching alternative for the patterning of niobium (Nb) and titanium nitride (TiN) thin films using the Standard Cleaning 1 (SC-1) solution. We characterize the etching process through its time-evolution dynamics, supported by scanning-electron and atomic force microscopy assessment of the etched film morphology. The results suggest etch dynamics that are linked to native oxides and film microstructure. Overall, the manageable etch rates, the safe operation and the high material selectivity are attractive for practical use in microelectronics fabrication.
summary: This paper presents a wet etching method using SC-1 solution as a damage-free alternative to dry etching for Nb and TiN thin films. While the process offers high selectivity and avoids plasma damage, the authors highlight challenges such as isotropic undercutting and morphology-dependent etch rates. This work is significant for the fabrication of high-quality superconducting and ferroelectric microelectronic components.
main_problem: The challenge of patterning Niobium (Nb) and Titanium Nitride (TiN) thin films without the plasma-induced damage and limited selectivity associated with standard dry etching techniques.
main_result: Demonstration of a highly selective SC-1 wet etching alternative that avoids substrate damage, though it is subject to isotropic undercutting and multi-modal etch rates driven by film morphology.
method: Wet etching using SC-1 solution (NH3, H2O2, and H2O) followed by characterization via SEM, AFM, and OpenCV-based image processing for surface area estimation.
model_or_system: Sputtered Nb and TiN thin films on 150 mm Si wafers with a thermally grown SiO2 layer and a PECVD SiO2 hard mask.
key_observables: Etch rates, film morphology (roughness, grain size, columnar structure), selectivity, and effective surface area increase.
important_parameters: Etch temperatures (65C for Nb, 45C for TiN), SC-1 volume ratio (1:1:5), and lateral cavity size due to overetching.
main_assumptions: The image processing approach assumes that SEM-derived contour measurements accurately represent the effective surface profile; AFM scanning was limited to specific regions to avoid tip damage.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the initial layer stack and the three stages of the etching process; Figure 3 displays cross-sectional SEM of films mid-etch; Figure S4 shows SEM-based contouring and AFM topography for surface area estimation; Figure S5 illustrates lateral cavity growth under the mask during overetching.
paper_structure: Introduction of the etching problem, description of the SC-1 experimental platform and method, analysis of Nb and TiN etch dynamics and morphology, quantification of surface area changes, and discussion of limitations like mask undercut.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: a) Cross-sectional SEM image of a chip at the edge of a mask opening prior to wet etching, showing the initial layer stack. b) Schematic of the progressive wet etching of a chip against time: (1) before etching, where the metal film is exposed within the SiO2 hard mask mask opening, (2) during etching, where an increase in the metal film roughness is observed, (3) after etching, where the metal film within the mask opening is fully etched and showing a potential overetching of the metal side-walls under the mask.

Figure 2: Etched depths estimated by cross-sectional SEM imaging as a function of etching time for the Nb (green circles) and TiN (blue squares) films. The grey-shaded horizontal areas show each film’s etching endpoint based on its estimated initial thickness. *The 8 nm/min rate is attributed to the NbOx etching, considering the interpretation given in text.
Figure 3: Cross-sectional SEM images of the etched area from the Nb and TiN films when the etching process is approximately half-way: after 80 s and 150 s for Nb and TiN, respectively.
